question:
Prove that for every positive integer $n$, there is a sequence of integers
$a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{2009}$ with $a_0 = 0$ and $a_{2009} = n$ such that each term
after $a_0$ is either an earlier term plus $2^k$ for some nonnegative integer $k$,
or of the form $b\,\mathrm{mod}\,c$ for some earlier positive terms $b$ and $c$.
[Here $b\,\mathrm{mod}\,c$ denotes the remainder when $b$ is divided by $c$,
so $0 \leq (b\,\mathrm{mod}\,c) < c$.]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}
solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
(based on work of Yufei Zhao)
Since any sequence of the desired form remains of the desired form upon multiplying each term by 2,
we may reduce to the case where $n$ is odd. In this case, take $x = 2^h$ for some
positive integer $h$ for which $x \geq n$, and set
\begin{align*}
a_0 &= 0\\
a_1 &= 1\\
a_2 &= 2x+1 = a_1 + 2x \\
a_3 &= (x+1)^2 = a_2 + x^2 \\
a_4 &= x^n+1 = a_1 + x^n\\
a_5 &= n(x+1) = a_4 \mod a_3\\
a_6 &= x \\
a_7 &= n = a_5 \mod a_6.
\end{align*}
We may pad the sequence to the desired length by taking
$a_8 = \cdots = a_{2009} = n$.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(by James Merryfield)
Suppose first that $n$ is not divisible by 3. Recall that since $2$ is a primitive root modulo
$3^2$, it is also a primitive root modulo $3^h$ for any positive integer $h$. In particular,
if we choose $h$ so that $3^{2h} > n$, then
there exists a positive integer $c$ for which $2^c \mod 3^{2h} = n$.
We now take $b$ to be a positive integer for which $2^b > 3^{2h}$, and then put
\begin{align*}
a_0 &= 0\\
a_1 &= 1\\
a_2 &= 3 = a_1 + 2\\
a_3 &= 3 + 2^b \\
a_4 &= 2^{2hb} \\
a_5 &= 3^{2h} = a_4 \mod a_3 \\
a_6 &= 2^c \\
a_7 &= n = a_6 \mod a_5.
\end{align*}
If $n$ is divisible by 3, we can force $a_7 = n-1$ as in the above
construction, then put $a_{8} = a_7 + 1 = n$. In both cases, we then pad the sequence
as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark.}
Hendrik Lenstra, Ronald van Luijk, and Gabriele Della Torre
suggest the following variant of the first solution requiring only 6 steps.
For $n$ odd and $x$ as in the first solution, set
\begin{align*}
a_0 &= 0\\
a_1 &= 1\\
a_2 &= x+1 = a_1 + x\\
a_3 &= x^n+x+1 = a_2 + x^n\\
a_4 &= x^{(n-1)(\phi(a_3)-1)}\\
a_5 &= \frac{x^n+1}{x+1} = a_4 \mod a_3 \\
a_6 &= n = a_5 \mod a_2.
\end{align*}
It seems unlikely that a shorter solution can be constructed without relying on
any deep number-theoretic conjectures.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
(based on work of Yufei Zhao)
Since any sequence of the desired form remains of the desired form upon multiplying each term by 2, we may reduce to the case where $targetnum$ is odd. In this case, take $powerbase = 2^{powheight}$ for some positive integer $powheight$ for which $powerbase \geq targetnum$, and set
\begin{align*}
startzero &= 0\\
firstval &= 1\\
secondval &= 2powerbase+1 = firstval + 2powerbase \\
thirdval &= (powerbase+1)^2 = secondval + powerbase^2 \\
fourthval &= powerbase^{targetnum}+1 = firstval + powerbase^{targetnum}\\
fifthval &= targetnum(powerbase+1) = fourthval \mod thirdval\\
sixthval &= powerbase \\
seventhval &= targetnum = fifthval \mod sixthval.
\end{align*}
We may pad the sequence to the desired length by taking $eighthval = \cdots = finalval = targetnum$.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(by James Merryfield)
Suppose first that $targetnum$ is not divisible by 3. Recall that since $2$ is a primitive root modulo $3^2$, it is also a primitive root modulo $3^{powheight}$ for any positive integer $powheight$. In particular, if we choose $powheight$ so that $3^{2powheight} > targetnum$, then there exists a positive integer $modconst$ for which $2^{modconst} \mod 3^{2powheight} = targetnum$. We now take $bigexpon$ to be a positive integer for which $2^{bigexpon} > 3^{2powheight}$, and then put
\begin{align*}
startzero &= 0\\
firstval &= 1\\
secondval &= 3 = firstval + 2\\
thirdval &= 3 + 2^{bigexpon} \\
fourthval &= 2^{2powheight bigexpon} \\
fifthval &= 3^{2powheight} = fourthval \mod thirdval \\
sixthval &= 2^{modconst} \\
seventhval &= targetnum = sixthval \mod fifthval.
\end{align*}
If $targetnum$ is divisible by 3, we can force $seventhval = targetnum-1$ as in the above construction, then put $eighthval = seventhval + 1 = targetnum$. In both cases, we then pad the sequence as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark.}
Hendrik Lenstra, Ronald van Luijk, and Gabriele Della Torre suggest the following variant of the first solution requiring only 6 steps. For $targetnum$ odd and $powerbase$ as in the first solution, set
\begin{align*}
startzero &= 0\\
firstval &= 1\\
secondval &= powerbase+1 = firstval + powerbase\\
thirdval &= powerbase^{targetnum}+powerbase+1 = secondval + powerbase^{targetnum}\\
fourthval &= powerbase^{(targetnum-1)(eulerphi(thirdval)-1)}\\
fifthval &= \frac{powerbase^{targetnum}+1}{powerbase+1} = fourthval \mod thirdval \\
sixthval &= targetnum = fifthval \mod secondval.
\end{align*}
It seems unlikely that a shorter solution can be constructed without relying on any deep number-theoretic conjectures.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that for every positive integer $porcupine$, there is a sequence of integers
$cloudberry, elderberry, \dots, cantaloupe$ with $cloudberry = 0$ and $cantaloupe = porcupine$ such that each term
after $cloudberry$ is either an earlier term plus $2^{windflower}$ for some nonnegative integer $windflower$,
or of the form $lemongrass\,\mathrm{mod}\,watercress$ for some earlier positive terms $lemongrass$ and $watercress$.
[Here $lemongrass\,\mathrm{mod}\,watercress$ denotes the remainder when $lemongrass$ is divided by $watercress$,
so $0 \leq (lemongrass\,\mathrm{mod}\,watercress) < watercress$.]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
(based on work of Yufei Zhao)
Since any sequence of the desired form remains of the desired form upon multiplying each term by 2,
we may reduce to the case where $porcupine$ is odd. In this case, take $tortoise = 2^{sandpiper}$ for some
positive integer $sandpiper$ for which $tortoise \geq porcupine$, and set
\begin{align*}
cloudberry &= 0\\
elderberry &= 1\\
passionfruit &= 2tortoise+1 = elderberry + 2tortoise \\
gooseberry &= (tortoise+1)^2 = passionfruit + tortoise^2 \\
pomegranate &= tortoise^{porcupine}+1 = elderberry + tortoise^{porcupine}\\
blackcurrant &= porcupine(tortoise+1) = pomegranate \mod gooseberry\\
persimmon &= tortoise \\
boysenberry &= porcupine = blackcurrant \mod persimmon.
\end{align*}
We may pad the sequence to the desired length by taking
$mulberry = \cdots = cantaloupe = porcupine$.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(by James Merryfield)
Suppose first that $porcupine$ is not divisible by 3. Recall that since $2$ is a primitive root modulo
$3^2$, it is also a primitive root modulo $3^{sandpiper}$ for any positive integer $sandpiper$. In particular,
if we choose $sandpiper$ so that $3^{2sandpiper} > porcupine$, then
there exists a positive integer $watercress$ for which $2^{watercress} \mod 3^{2sandpiper} = porcupine$.
We now take $lemongrass$ to be a positive integer for which $2^{lemongrass} > 3^{2sandpiper}$, and then put
\begin{align*}
cloudberry &= 0\\
elderberry &= 1\\
passionfruit &= 3 = elderberry + 2\\
gooseberry &= 3 + 2^{lemongrass} \\
pomegranate &= 2^{2 sandpiper lemongrass} \\
blackcurrant &= 3^{2sandpiper} = pomegranate \mod gooseberry \\
persimmon &= 2^{watercress} \\
boysenberry &= porcupine = persimmon \mod blackcurrant.
\end{align*}
If $porcupine$ is divisible by 3, we can force $boysenberry = porcupine-1$ as in the above
construction, then put $mulberry = boysenberry + 1 = porcupine$. In both cases, we then pad the sequence
as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark.}
Hendrik Lenstra, Ronald van Luijk, and Gabriele Della Torre
suggest the following variant of the first solution requiring only 6 steps.
For $porcupine$ odd and $tortoise$ as in the first solution, set
\begin{align*}
cloudberry &= 0\\
elderberry &= 1\\
passionfruit &= tortoise+1 = elderberry + tortoise\\
gooseberry &= tortoise^{porcupine}+tortoise+1 = passionfruit + tortoise^{porcupine}\\
pomegranate &= tortoise^{(porcupine-1)(kingfisher(gooseberry)-1)}\\
blackcurrant &= \frac{tortoise^{porcupine}+1}{tortoise+1} = pomegranate \mod gooseberry \\
boysenberry &= porcupine = blackcurrant \mod passionfruit.
\end{align*}
It seems unlikely that a shorter solution can be constructed without relying on
any deep number-theoretic conjectures.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Prove that for every positive integer $negativity$, there is a sequence of integers
$endpoint, ultimate, \dots, neverstart$ with $endpoint = 0$ and $neverstart = negativity$ such that each term
after $endpoint$ is either an earlier term plus $2^{basepart}$ for some nonnegative integer $basepart$,
or of the form $littlebit\,\mathrm{mod}\,voidness$ for some earlier positive terms $littlebit$ and $voidness$.
[Here $littlebit\,\mathrm{mod}\,voidness$ denotes the remainder when $littlebit$ is divided by $voidness$,
so $0 \leq (littlebit\,\mathrm{mod}\,voidness) < voidness$.]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
(based on work of Yufei Zhao)
Since any sequence of the desired form remains of the desired form upon multiplying each term by 2,
we may reduce to the case where $negativity$ is odd. In this case, take $minuscule = 2^{magnitude}$ for some
positive integer $magnitude$ for which $minuscule \geq negativity$, and set
\begin{align*}
endpoint &= 0\\
ultimate &= 1\\
postfinal &= 2minuscule+1 = ultimate + 2minuscule \\
postfinish &= (minuscule+1)^2 = postfinal + minuscule^2 \\
overfinish &= minuscule^{negativity}+1 = ultimate + minuscule^{negativity}\\
beyondend &= negativity(minuscule+1) = overfinish \mod postfinish\\
laststage &= minuscule \\
terminus &= negativity = beyondend \mod laststage.
\end{align*}
We may pad the sequence to the desired length by taking
$climaxzone = \cdots = neverstart = negativity$.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(by James Merryfield)
Suppose first that $negativity$ is not divisible by 3. Recall that since $2$ is a primitive root modulo
$3^2$, it is also a primitive root modulo $3^{magnitude}$ for any positive integer $magnitude$. In particular,
if we choose $magnitude$ so that $3^{2magnitude} > negativity$, then
there exists a positive integer $voidness$ for which $2^{voidness} \mod 3^{2magnitude} = negativity$.
We now take $littlebit$ to be a positive integer for which $2^{littlebit} > 3^{2magnitude}$, and then put
\begin{align*}
endpoint &= 0\\
ultimate &= 1\\
postfinal &= 3 = ultimate + 2\\
postfinish &= 3 + 2^{littlebit} \\
overfinish &= 2^{2 magnitude littlebit} \\
beyondend &= 3^{2magnitude} = overfinish \mod postfinish \\
laststage &= 2^{voidness} \\
terminus &= negativity = laststage \mod beyondend.
\end{align*}
If $negativity$ is divisible by 3, we can force $terminus = negativity-1$ as in the above
construction, then put $climaxzone = terminus + 1 = negativity$. In both cases, we then pad the sequence
as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark.}
Hendrik Lenstra, Ronald van Luijk, and Gabriele Della Torre
suggest the following variant of the first solution requiring only 6 steps.
For $negativity$ odd and $minuscule$ as in the first solution, set
\begin{align*}
endpoint &= 0\\
ultimate &= 1\\
postfinal &= minuscule+1 = ultimate + minuscule\\
postfinish &= minuscule^{negativity}+minuscule+1 = postfinal + minuscule^{negativity}\\
overfinish &= minuscule^{(negativity-1)(noncoprime(postfinish)-1)}\\
beyondend &= \frac{minuscule^{negativity}+1}{minuscule+1} = overfinish \mod postfinish \\
laststage &= negativity = beyondend \mod postfinal.
\end{align*}
It seems unlikely that a shorter solution can be constructed without relying on
any deep number-theoretic conjectures.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

garbled_string.question:
Prove that for every positive integer $ghsdvrke$, there is a sequence of integers
$qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, \dots, zfplqwdm$ with $qzxwvtnp = 0$ and $zfplqwdm = ghsdvrke$ such that each term
after $qzxwvtnp$ is either an earlier term plus $2^{jhvlspdo}$ for some nonnegative integer $jhvlspdo$,
or of the form $xdgklvtr\,\mathrm{mod}\,rqspmnhd$ for some earlier positive terms $xdgklvtr$ and $rqspmnhd$.
[Here $xdgklvtr\,\mathrm{mod}\,rqspmnhd$ denotes the remainder when $xdgklvtr$ is divided by $rqspmnhd$,
so $0 \leq (xdgklvtr\,\mathrm{mod}\,rqspmnhd) < rqspmnhd$.]
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
(based on work of Yufei Zhao)
Since any sequence of the desired form remains of the desired form upon multiplying each term by 2,
we may reduce to the case where $ghsdvrke$ is odd. In this case, take $lrqtmnvy = 2^{wqztnmpr}$ for some
positive integer $wqztnmpr$ for which $lrqtmnvy \geq ghsdvrke$, and set
\begin{align*}
qzxwvtnp &= 0\\
hjgrksla &= 1\\
pqlmznxy &= 2lrqtmnvy+1 = hjgrksla + 2lrqtmnvy \\
dvhsakjt &= (lrqtmnvy+1)^2 = pqlmznxy + lrqtmnvy^2 \\
mnygqzrb &= lrqtmnvy^{ghsdvrke}+1 = hjgrksla + lrqtmnvy^{ghsdvrke}\\
tcfklswe &= ghsdvrke(lrqtmnvy+1) = mnygqzrb \mod dvhsakjt\\
vblxqjrh &= lrqtmnvy \\
sgzmtrpy &= ghsdvrke = tcfklswe \mod vblxqjrh.
\end{align*}
We may pad the sequence to the desired length by taking
kldsnwqe = \cdots = zfplqwdm = ghsdvrke.

\textbf{Second solution.}
(by James Merryfield)
Suppose first that $ghsdvrke$ is not divisible by 3. Recall that since $2$ is a primitive root modulo
$3^2$, it is also a primitive root modulo $3^{wqztnmpr}$ for any positive integer $wqztnmpr$. In particular,
if we choose $wqztnmpr$ so that $3^{2 wqztnmpr} > ghsdvrke$, then
there exists a positive integer $rqspmnhd$ for which $2^{rqspmnhd} \mod 3^{2 wqztnmpr} = ghsdvrke$.
We now take $xdgklvtr$ to be a positive integer for which $2^{xdgklvtr} > 3^{2 wqztnmpr}$, and then put
\begin{align*}
qzxwvtnp &= 0\\
hjgrksla &= 1\\
pqlmznxy &= 3 = hjgrksla + 2\\
dvhsakjt &= 3 + 2^{xdgklvtr} \\
mnygqzrb &= 2^{2 wqztnmpr xdgklvtr} \\
tcfklswe &= 3^{2 wqztnmpr} = mnygqzrb \mod dvhsakjt \\
vblxqjrh &= 2^{rqspmnhd} \\
sgzmtrpy &= ghsdvrke = vblxqjrh \mod tcfklswe.
\end{align*}
If $ghsdvrke$ is divisible by 3, we can force $sgzmtrpy = ghsdvrke-1$ as in the above
construction, then put $kldsnwqe = sgzmtrpy + 1 = ghsdvrke$. In both cases, we then pad the sequence
as in the first solution.

\textbf{Remark.}
Hendrik Lenstra, Ronald van Luijk, and Gabriele Della Torre
suggest the following variant of the first solution requiring only 6 steps.
For $ghsdvrke$ odd and $lrqtmnvy$ as in the first solution, set
\begin{align*}
qzxwvtnp &= 0\\
hjgrksla &= 1\\
pqlmznxy &= lrqtmnvy+1 = hjgrksla + lrqtmnvy\\
dvhsakjt &= lrqtmnvy^{ghsdvrke}+lrqtmnvy+1 = pqlmznxy + lrqtmnvy^{ghsdvrke}\\
mnygqzrb &= lrqtmnvy^{(ghsdvrke-1)(lkdjvhru(dvhsakjt)-1)}\\
tcfklswe &= \frac{lrqtmnvy^{ghsdvrke}+1}{lrqtmnvy+1} = mnygqzrb \mod dvhsakjt \\
vblxqjrh &= ghsdvrke = tcfklswe \mod pqlmznxy.
\end{align*}
It seems unlikely that a shorter solution can be constructed without relying on
any deep number-theoretic conjectures.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n$ be a positive integer.  Prove that there exists a sequence of integers
\[b_0,b_1,\dots ,b_{2024}\]
with $b_0=0$ and $b_{2024}=n$ such that for every $j>0$ the term $b_j$ is obtained from 
some earlier terms in one of the following two ways:
\begin{itemize}
\item $b_j=b_i+2^k$ for some $i<j$ and some non-negative integer $k$, or
\item $b_j=b_i\bmod b_{\ell}$ for some $i,\ell<j$ with $b_{\ell}>0$.
\end{itemize}
(Here $x\bmod y$ denotes the remainder on dividing $x$ by $y$, so $0\le x\bmod y<y$.)
kernel_variant.solution:
We prove by construction that for any positive integer n there is a sequence b_0,\ldots ,b_{2024} with b_0=0, b_{2024}=n, and each b_j (j>0) obtained either as an earlier term plus a power of two or as a remainder of one earlier term modulo another.

Step 1. Reduction to n odd.
Write n=2^s\cdot n_0 with n_0 odd.  By induction assume we have built a sequence ending at n_0.  Multiplying every term by 2^s preserves both allowed moves (adding 2^k becomes adding 2^{k+s}, and taking x mod y becomes (x\cdot 2^s) mod (y\cdot 2^s)), so we obtain a valid sequence ending at n.  Hence from now on assume n is odd.

Step 2. Choose a large power of two.
Let h be an integer with 2^h > n^2, and set x = 2^h.  In particular x > n.

Step 3. Build auxiliary terms by +2^k moves.
b_0 = 0,

b_1 = 1,

b_2 = 2x + 1 = b_1 + 2\cdot x,

b_3 = (x+1)^2 = b_2 + x^2,

b_4 = x^n + 1 = b_1 + x^n.

Here 2x, x^2, x^n are all powers of two, so each step is of the form ``previous term + 2^k.''

Step 4. Extract n via two modulo moves.
By the binomial theorem (and n odd) we have
  x^n + 1 \equiv  n(x+1)  (mod (x+1)^2).
Hence define
  b_5 = b_4 mod b_3 = (x^n + 1) mod (x+1)^2 = n(x+1).

Next set
  b_6 = x = b_0 + x  
(a valid +2^k move).
Then
  b_7 = b_5 mod b_6 = n(x+1) mod x = n,
since n(x+1)=nx + n and n < x.

Step 5. Pad to length 2025 by modulo moves.
For each j = 8,9,\ldots ,2024 define
  b_j = b_7 mod b_6.
Because b_7 = n < x = b_6, we have b_j = n each time, and taking a remainder of an earlier term by an earlier larger term is allowed.

Final check.  We have produced b_0,\ldots ,b_{2024} with b_0=0, b_{2024}=n, and every step either adds a power of two or takes a remainder, as required.  This completes the proof.

core_steps: Remove even factors: multiply any admissible sequence by 2 so only the case n odd has to be built
Choose a power of two x = 2^h â‰Ą n so every needed +2^k jump is available
Via +2^k moves create the numbers 1, x+1, (x+1)^2, and x^n+1
Apply mod-steps: (x^n+1) mod (x+1)^2 = n(x+1) and then [n(x+1)] mod x = n
Pad with repeated nâ€™s to reach the prescribed sequence length

slot_length.description: Total required length of the sequence; any value â‰Ą 7 lets the padding trick work

slot_x_power.description: Power-of-two bound chosen for x; only the inequality x â‰Ą n is needed
